---
title: "Water Fern (Azolla filiculoides)"
slug: "azolla-filiculoides"
group: "Plantae"
image: "https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/476625269/medium.jpeg"        # keep for thumbnails
image-alt: "Water Fern"  # keep for a11y
tax_kingdom: "Plantae"
tax_class: "Polypodiopsida"
tax_order: "Salviniales"
tax_family: "Salviniaceae"
categories: ["Plantae", "Polypodiopsida", "Salviniales", "Salviniaceae"]   # these drive the chips + filtering
freeze: true
---

<figure class="species-figure">

<img src="https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/476625269/medium.jpeg" alt="Water Fern" class="species-photo"/>

<figcaption class="photo-credit">

Photo: <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/people/megankimreid">
megankimreid</a> (CC BY) via <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/265357078">iNaturalist</a>

</figcaption>

</figure>

## Taxonomy

-   **Class:** Polypodiopsida
-   **Order:** Salviniales
-   **Family:** Salviniaceae

## Occurrence at DeLuca Preserve

-   [DeLuca Bio Blitz Species Observations](https://www.inaturalist.org/observations?project_id=191727&subview=map&taxon_id=58754)

## Conservation & rarity context

-   IUCN: Not Listed
-   **Rarity note:** Common to Florida and found in still water.

## Natural history notes

A small, free-floating aquatic fern characterized by its tiny overlapping leaves that often turn reddish in bright sunlight. In Florida, it is found on the surface of still or धी slow-moving water such as ponds, ditches, and wetlands. It reproduces by spores and can rapidly form dense mats, providing cover for aquatic organisms.

## References / links

-   [iNaturalist species page](https://www.inaturalist.org/taxa/58754-Azolla-filiculoides)
-   [Local profile](https://florida.plantatlas.usf.edu/plant/species/2220)
